Just now

--

Moving beyond probabilistic guessing to build an AI that actually understands the world.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated by AIWhat is the problem?
The current version of AI “works” on probabilistic calculus. Is it worth describing that this has its pros? It is enough to use any chat to make sure — it works, and it works quite well. Probabilistic calculus works so well that humanity even experiences sometimes timid, sometimes passionate hopes of one day seeing a full-fledged artificial intelligence…
Probability has many problems. The main one is that probability never describes “what is happening”, it only says “what should happen”, and then, relying on the mathematical trick of discarding infinitesimals, simply accepts that “what happened” is equal to “what should happen”. Indeed, if you predicted that a sandwich would fall, and the sandwich fell, what difference does it make to you that the theory on which the prediction was based might be wrong?
However, in this article, we will not criticize probability theory itself. It works, and it works quite well. Here, we are interested in where AI will go next. Okay, we have an AI that guesses based on probability that “love” is followed by “you”. But we would like an AI that does not guess, but knows. Can AI “know” or “understand” something?
And again, let’s not get into a discussion. We will briefly note that AI is not capable of understanding. Understanding is the ability to operate with contradictions. AI cannot operate with contradictions. However, AI is capable of helping a person to understand. And it is exactly this kind of AI that will replace the current one.
So, the task is: take the maximum that AI is capable of, and find the point in the algorithm of understanding the world where AI can help a person with this “maximum”. The limit for AI is set by the limit of computer operations, and this limit is the inability to work with contradictions. At the same time, understanding the world is precisely the ability to work with contradictions.
What do we get? Is AI unable to help us in the only thing that truly matters — understanding the world? It is capable, but we need to change our approach. Currently, we strive to avoid contradictions, but what should we do instead? We must “build” the functioning of computers and AI in such a way that contradictions become their natural environment. This is exactly what requires a slightly different mathematics. An attempt to outline the basics of this mathematics is provided below. I will immediately note that while this mathematics has reached the core problem, it has not yet provided a solution: it is clear how to qualitatively describe an ordinary world full of contradictions using mathematics, but how to transition from this mathematical, qualitative description to a quantitative one — a solution for this has not yet been found.
Hash Mathematics
Why a hash? Because a “hash” is a “compression” (or convolution) of any, even an infinite amount of data, into a short label. Data can be compared using such labels, and information about the structure of the world can be extracted from this comparison.
Here are the basic principles of hash mathematics.

• Everything in the world is perceived as a process. Whether it is text, the movement of a car, feelings, experiences, stones on the road, poetry, medical documents — all of this, and everything in general, is perceived as a process.

• Any process can be described by a hash.

• A hash is formed by an arbitrarily set label divided by the sum of processes that reveal the hash (revealing processes). Here, perhaps, we need to pause and take a slightly more detailed look.
Example:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here the hashes appear, here they are:
and
The two series coincide in infinity, that is, N=M, but the labels do not coincide (n is not equal to m). This is the meaning of the notation — to show dialectics through the limited means of mathematics, that is, through summation.
4. Series coincide in infinity, but labels do not. However, hashes coincide in infinity.
5. Although hashes coincide in infinity, they diverge as they move towards infinity, except for those that describe opposites. This creates the possibility of finding opposites.
Let’s digress a little from the attempt to formalize hashes. What is this about? Take any process, say, reading an article on the Internet. This process has many “properties” — other processes: you opened the page, scrolled through the feed, chose an article; a long time ago you learned to read; you even went to school to understand mathematics.
Each such relatively closed process is a “unit” in hash mathematics. The totality of such units is actually the initial process of “reading an article on the Internet”. Let’s stipulate that this is far from classical dialectics, but remember our task: to take non-dialectical mathematics and somehow link it to the dialectical world.
So, the totality of unit-properties creates a process. Okay, that is clear, but what does the rest mean? Cognition is infinite, therefore, for example, it is impossible to completely understand why you decided to read this article. Thus, the units are summed infinitely. However, even though an infinite number of reasons led you to read this article, there is absolutely no need to know this entire infinity to simply say — you are reading this article. Therefore, the infinite sum of units has a well-defined label — 2.
But since we have obtained a summation and a label, we can also define a hash, thereby “individualizing” our process. What does this give us? We can find another process with a hash and see if there is a match between the two. Is there? Then we are looking at opposites, and this provides contradictions, which in turn provides understanding…
Yes, for now, the entire last part looks more like a utopia. So far, I have not been able to solve this problem of comparing two hashes. After all, for example, two hashes with initial units are already equal. Yes, we are actually talking about infinity, so we don’t take the initial units into account… In short, more thought is required here.
Literally on the day I was finishing this article, I had an idea that comparing two hashes might be possible by looking at the density of processes calculated in a certain way. Perhaps this idea will become the solution, or perhaps just a stepping stone to it.
Why is this necessary?
If we could “calculate” opposites, we would be in luck. See for yourself. The entire world is movement and development. But development implies contradiction, the emergence of a contradiction, and the overcoming of a contradiction. A computer cannot work with contradictions, but it can work with opposites. It is quite fortunate that contradictions begin with opposites.
Thus, we resolve the contradiction indicated above: we can combine AI and understanding through that very special mathematics outlined above. But this means that we can truly understand the world, rather than just guessing about it.
This, of course, sounds quite ambitious. But here is the question. Suppose the current AI answers “four” to the question “what is two times two”, and our “advanced” AI answers the same question. What will this second one answer? Well, “four”, of course. So what is the difference? One guessed, the other can justify it, but what is the actual difference for the “end consumer”, so to speak?
First, we would be able to answer questions for which ordinary AI lacks data. After all, two times two is four for current AI only because it is written that way in most books. But what if certain topics are poorly covered in those books? Take dialectics. There are very few books on it; literature on dialectical physics, for example, is almost non-existent. How can you use AI to answer what movement is? Current AI will say anything about movement, except what dialectics actually says about it.
Second, AI currently needs to be trained. We throw a huge net over the world and then adjust it, trying to figure out what is “growing” and where. This is a costly path. When you don’t have a picture of the world “in your head”, you waste a massive amount of effort on trial and error. This is how current AI operates, which may suit video card manufacturers and data center builders, but does it suit everyone else?
The presented “hash calculus” is a picture of the world that actually has to work. I am already developing the papanda service, which is designed precisely to utilize this second-generation AI. Ideally, an AI that does not require complex training will be able to work efficiently locally on the user’s computer, managing data without relying on cloud computing power.
And here the key word is “has to”. It has to, but will it? It is quite probable. In any case, current AI will definitely stop satisfying people in a year or two. We need to look for a new AI, and it is better to start this search in advance.
P.S. I would like to dedicate this article to a certain person who does not even know yet that I am “inventing” this algorithm for him. Dedicated.
